mcq.q: How did Louise realise she had been robbed?
opts: Her wallet was missing, and then she found her phone had gone too
Someone told her at the museum

mcq.q: What was the main suspect wearing?
opts: A T-shirt with a tiger picture

1.errorText: A woman called Louise Taylor visits the police station to report a theft. She is Canadian, though her mother was British. She is an interior designer and comes to the UK to buy old furniture. She is staying in the Riverside Apartments at number fifteen on King Street. She had a backpack on her back and went into a supermarket when her wallet went missing. The wallet contained about £300 in cash. Her phone had also been taken — it was only a week old. The theft took place on September 10th. She thinks it happened near the cinema at about 4 o'clock, when a group of boys crowded around her and asked what the date was. The main suspect was blond and wearing a T-shirt with a tiger picture, and was about twelve years old.

pragmatic.quote: More than half the world's population lives in cities now — though that's all cities, not just ones on the coast.
pragmatic.speaker: Carla

pragmatic.quote: A lot of cities are built where rivers come out into the sea, and as the city expands, more pollutants are discharged into the rivers and brought downstream.
pragmatic.speaker: Rob

pragmatic.quote: They built drainage channels too close to sea level, and now sea levels are rising — if there's a lot of rain, there's nowhere for the water to go. The whole design was faulty.
pragmatic.speaker: Rob

pragmatic.quote: Really, coastal cities can't deal with their problems on their own — they've got to start acting at an international level instead of just doing their own thing.
pragmatic.speaker: Carla

pragmatic.quote: It's going to be a long time before countries come to a decision on what principles they're prepared to abide by — if they ever do.
pragmatic.speaker: Rob

signpost.quote: Demand is rising rapidly, because of the world's increasing population and expanding industry.

opts: Stating the key advantage of wave energy before introducing its limitations

signpost.quote: One major advantage of using the tide, rather than waves, as a source of energy is that it's predictable.
opts: Explicitly contrasting tidal energy's key advantage against the wave energy discussed above

signpost.quote: This system has quite a lot in its favour: unlike solar and wind energy it doesn't depend on the weather.
opts: Summarising the benefits of the tidal lagoon system after describing how it works

signpost.quote: On the other hand, there are fears that lagoons might harm both fish and birds, for example by disturbing migration patterns.
opts: Introducing environmental concerns after listing economic and operational benefits

Today I'll outline marine renewable energy — which harnesses the movement of the oceans. It can be divided into three main categories: wave energy, tidal energy and ocean thermal energy conversion.

, and there's no danger of waves stopping. But the problem is that waves are erratic, with the wind making them travel in every
Another drawback is that sand and other sediment on the ocean
might be stopped from flowing normally, which can lead to environmental problems.
One major advantage of tidal energy over waves is that it's
— we know the exact times of high and low tides for years to come.
One plan is to create a tidal lagoon — an enclosed area of water within a
at Swansea, Wales, sheltered by a U-shaped breakwater containing sixteen turbines.
As the tide rises, water rushes through the breakwater activating the turbines. Then the water is held back. When the
in the breakwater are opened, the stored water pours out, driving the turbines in the opposite direction.
The system operates without the need for
, so it doesn't create greenhouse gas emissions. Manufacturing the components would create more than 2,000
On the other hand, there are fears that lagoons might harm both fish and birds — for example by disturbing

She works full-time as a waitress at Esterhazy's restaurant chain. Her insurance company is Cawley Life Insurance. She has been getting pain in her right knee for about two weeks. Last year she slipped and hurt her hip.

4.scene: 天气预报早期历史讲座

signpost.quote: Most ancient cultures had weather gods, and weather catastrophes, such as floods, played an important role in many creation myths.
opts: Establishing the mythological context before describing the shift to scientific observation

signpost.quote: The ancient Greeks were the first to develop a more scientific approach to explaining the weather.
opts: Marking the transition from myth and observation to the beginnings of scientific theory

signpost.quote: In the Middle Ages, weather observations were passed on in the form of proverbs, alongside Aristotle's work.
opts: Noting a parallel popular tradition existing alongside formal scholarly theory

signpost.quote: In the fifteenth century scientists began to see the need for instruments. Until then the only ones available were weather vanes and early versions of rain gauges.
opts: Introducing a pivotal development — the shift from observation to measurement

signpost.quote: This increasing ability to measure factors related to weather helped scientists to understand the atmosphere and its processes better.
opts: Connecting the invention of instruments to systematic data collection and eventually the first weather services

to please the weather gods

mindmap.title: Aristotle (340 BC)
items: Wrote about rain,
, wind and storms — accurate on heat causing evaporation
Also described celestial phenomena — comets, haloes
His ideas held sway for nearly 2,000 years; errors rectified from the
onwards

mindmap.title: Renaissance Instruments
items: 15th century: hygrometer invented — measured humidity
1592: Galileo developed the world's first
Torricelli (Galileo's student): invented the barometer — measured atmospheric pressure
Pascal (1648): proved pressure decreases with altitude; verified by Halley in 1686
Halley: also the first to map trade winds

mindmap.title: 18th–19th Century
items: Benjamin Franklin: worked on electricity and lightning; discovered
generally travel west to east
Mid-19th century: invention of the
allowed records in different locations to be collated
By end of 19th century: first
services established

in order to make the weather gods look kindly on them.

depended on it. Around 650 BC, the Babylonians produced the first short-range weather forecasts based on observations of
The Chinese developed a calendar dividing the year into 24

The ancient Greeks were the first to develop a more scientific approach. Aristotle, in 340 BC, attempted to account for the formation of rain, clouds, wind and storms. He also described
, such as haloes around the sun.
Aristotle believed that heat could cause water to evaporate. But he also made errors — like saying that winds are breathed out by the Earth. These were rectified from the Renaissance onwards.
In the Middle Ages, weather observations were passed on as proverbs, like 'Red
at night, shepherd's delight'. Many are based on good observations and are accurate.

. One of the first was a hygrometer, which measured humidity.

. His student Torricelli later invented the barometer.

generally travel from west to east.

made it possible for weather records to be collated. This led, by the end of the century, to the first weather services.

pragmatic.quote: The town council has asked the band to perform at the carnival in the summer — I need to get them functioning better as a band, and in a very short time.
pragmatic.speaker: Joe

pragmatic.quote: I played a recording of a drummer talking about how playing in a band had stopped him getting involved in crime. I thought it was pretty inspiring.
pragmatic.speaker: Joe

pragmatic.quote: There's a trumpeter who thinks she's the best musician in the band — she's always saying what she thinks other people should do, which makes my job difficult.
pragmatic.speaker: Joe

pragmatic.quote: One of the percussionists never seems to interact with other people — he always rushes off as soon as the rehearsal ends.

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: The theme of the festival is links between the UK and Australia. Each concert will feature both British and Australian composers. I'll tell you about the Australian music, as you probably won't be familiar with that.
Liza Lim was born in Perth in 1966. As a child she learned to play the piano and also the
. When she was 11, her teachers encouraged her to start composing.
Lim's compositions are vibrant and full of
. She often explores Asian and Australian Aboriginal cultural sources, including the didgeridoo.
In the festival we'll give a semi-staged performance of 'The Oresteia' — an opera in seven parts based on ancient Greek
. Lim wrote it when she was in her mid-20s.
Because the stories in the tragedies are not easy to tell, the sounds she creates are also
— including breathing, sobbing, laughing and whistling.
Concert 2 features 'The Tower of Remoteness' by Ross Edwards — scored for
and clarinet. Its inspiration came from nature: sitting alone in the dry bed of a creek, listening to birds and insects.
Edwards was born in Sydney in 1943. His music has been described as 'deeply connected to Australia' — a celebration of the
of cultures that Australia can be proud of.
Carl Vine comes from Perth and took up the cornet at five, switching to piano five years later. He went to university to study
, before changing to composition.
After graduating he moved to Sydney. Before long he had become prominent as a composer for
— he has written 25 scores of that type.
In Concert 3, Vine is represented by his music for the 1996
flag hand-over ceremony — heard by millions of people worldwide.

1.scene: 克莱尔庄园酒店周年晚宴咨询

mcq.q: What is the capacity of the Adelphi Room for a sit-down meal?
What does the Carlton Room have that the Adelphi Room does not?
opts: A stage for entertainment or a small band

mcq.q: What is the MC's role, according to Andrew?
opts: To organise the menu and catering
To make a speech, manage the event, and deal with anything that goes wrong

1.errorText: Samantha calls the Clare House Hotel to arrange a party for her parents' fiftieth wedding anniversary. Andrew is the Events Manager. The Adelphi Room can seat 85 people or hold over 100 standing. If there is live music, there is room for four or five musicians in the gallery. The room is at the back of the hotel, with French windows leading to a terrace displaying a beautiful collection of pots of dahlias. From the terrace you can see trees in the grounds and a river on the far side. The Carlton Room is bigger and has a stage, but it has no outdoor access and its windows look out onto a garden. The hotel also has five cabins in the grounds, each sleeping two to four people. The pool will be closed in September, but the courts are available for a small additional charge.

errors.correct: five cabins ✓ — replace: 'pots of dahlias' with 'pots of roses' done

errors.wrong: the courts are available for free
errors.correct: the courts have a small additional charge

errors.wrong: the gym is available for a small charge
errors.correct: the gym is available at no extra charge

3.scene: 儿童文学课程讨论与书籍交流
3.functions: A  Expressing enthusiasm for a module    B  Giving a personal opinion on a book    C  Agreeing with a point after initial disagreement    D  Describing a personal creative response    E  Expressing regret or criticism

pragmatic.quote: That module made me realise how important stories are — they can have a significant effect on children as they grow up. It inspired me to have a go at it myself.
pragmatic.speaker: Trevor

pragmatic.quote: I'm very interested in illustrations in stories. I can still see some frightening ones vividly in my mind, years later — pictures can be so powerful.
pragmatic.speaker: Stephanie

pragmatic.quote: I'm not convinced of comics' value — with a comic you don't have to use your imagination, but with words you do.
pragmatic.speaker: Trevor

pragmatic.quote: It's better to read a comic than not to read at all — there are plenty of kids who wouldn't try to read a book at all.
pragmatic.speaker: Stephanie

pragmatic.quote: The Happy Prince is really moving — so surprising because Wilde is best known for his witty plays, but this is quite different.
pragmatic.speaker: Stephanie

signpost.quote: Atlit-Yam was a village on the coast of the eastern Mediterranean, which seems to have been thriving until around 7,000 BC.
opts: Introducing a specific case study to anchor the lecture before broadening to technology

signpost.quote: For marine archaeologists, Atlit-Yam is a treasure trove. Research on the buildings, tools and human remains has revealed how the bustling village once functioned.
opts: Showing the research value of the site before identifying the challenge of finding more

signpost.quote: Autonomous underwater vehicles, or AUVs, are used in the oil industry, for instance, to create maps of the seabed before rigs and pipelines are installed.
opts: Introducing AUVs in an existing non-archaeological context before describing their archaeological potential

signpost.quote: The latest AUVs are much easier to manoeuvre — they can be launched from the shore or a small ship. And they're much cheaper, which makes them more accessible to research teams.
opts: Contrasting current AUVs with their earlier, more expensive and cumbersome predecessors

signpost.quote: Creating an internet in the sea for AUVs to communicate is no easy matter. Wifi networks on land use electromagnetic waves, but in water these will only travel a few centimetres.
opts: Identifying the fundamental technical challenge before explaining the solutions

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: Atlit-Yam was a village on the coast of the eastern Mediterranean, thriving until around 7,000 BC. Residents kept cattle, caught fish and stored grain. The village contained an impressive monument: seven half-tonne stones in a semicircle around a
It now lies ten metres below the Mediterranean. Research on the buildings,
and human remains has revealed how the village once functioned.
AUVs are used in the oil industry to create
of the seabed before rigs and pipelines are installed. Until recently they were very expensive and so heavy that they had to be launched with a winch from a large vessel.
The latest AUVs are much easier to manoeuvre and much cheaper — launchable from the shore. They can communicate with each other to find the most efficient way to survey a site.
Creating an internet in the sea is no easy matter. Wifi uses electromagnetic waves, but in water these will only travel a few centimetres. Instead, for short distances, AUVs share data using
For long distances, acoustic waves are used. Or an AUV on the seabed can pass data to a second AUV which surfaces and beams it home via
If an AUV finds an interesting object on the seabed, it can share the coordinates with a nearby AUV that carries superior
to make a closer inspection.
In the Gulf of Baratti, a 2,000-year-old Roman vessel was discovered in 18 metres of water. Its cargo included
goods — including tablets thought to have been dissolved to form a cleansing liquid for the
Other nearby Roman ships sank while carrying huge terracotta pots — some used for olive oil, others for
. In many cases only these containers remain.
